text: The robotic camera reveals omental attachments and a large, tense gallbladder distended with stones.
The gallbladder appears less tense following aspiration, although minimal fluid was aspirated due to the gallbladder being densely packed with stones.
The patient's liver is visibly enlarged, with the left hepatic lobe extending across to the right side, covering the gallbladder infundibulum, presenting a challenging surgical field.
The surgeon attempts to use ICG and Firefly imaging to identify the cystic duct structures, but no structures are immediately visible.

The surgeon begins by dissecting the peritoneum on the lateral aspect of the gallbladder, progressing upwards and then across, carefully cutting through the inflamed peritoneal tissue to reach the medial aspect.

The surgeon attempts to grasp the tense and stone-filled gallbladder multiple times, encountering difficulty due to its rigidity. The dissection progresses towards the medial aspect, stripping down the thick, inflamed peritoneum surrounding the gallbladder, indicative of severe acute cholecystitis.
The surgeon uses Firefly and ICG imaging to visualize the cystic duct structures, with the common bile duct and duodenum visible in the lower part of the field.
The surgeon utilizes Firefly mode with ICG to enhance visualization during dissection, effectively clearing inflammatory tissue around the gallbladder.
The surgeon carefully dissects the cystic artery, separating it from surrounding inflamed tissue using robotic instruments.
The surgeon uses a robotic grasper to hold the infundibulum of the gallbladder. The Firefly mode with ICG highlights the cystic duct, facilitating the dissection of surrounding inflammatory tissue.
The surgeon switches between white light and Firefly mode during the robotic cholecystectomy, enhancing visibility of anatomical structures and inflammatory tissue.
The surgeon navigates through the triangle of Calot, encountering significant inflammatory tissue while dissecting the cystic artery free from the adjacent cystic duct.
The surgeon persistently and carefully manipulates the inflamed gallbladder tissue in the setting of acute cholecystitis, using robotic assistance to navigate the challenging anatomy.
In Firefly mode, the surgeon identifies the cystic duct clearly and works to dissect the cystic artery from surrounding tissue, aiming to achieve a critical view of safety.
The surgeon clears inflammatory tissue around the cystic duct and cystic artery, creating space and exposing the first third of the hepatic cystic plate.
The surgeon uses scissors to clean up inflammatory tissue around the cystic duct, ensuring enough space is available to place clips on the duct.

The surgeon navigates around the backside of the cystic artery, identifying the critical view of safety with the liver visible through the artery and duct. The dissection focuses on freeing the first third of the cystic plate to ensure clear identification of structures for clipping.

The surgeon uses Firefly imaging to confirm the identification of the cystic duct leading into the common bile duct amidst dense inflammation. The gallbladder appears inflamed, and the surgeon prepares to apply clips to the identified structures.
The surgeon places two clips on the cystic duct and one clip proximally, securing the duct in preparation for transection.

The surgeon ligates and cuts the cystic duct, which has been controlled and clipped. The cystic artery is also taken down. The gallbladder, described as inflamed, is detached from the liver using monopolar energy through scissors.
The surgeon uses electrocautery to dissect the inflamed gallbladder, with visible smoke being evacuated from the surgical field.
The surgeon completes the detachment of the gallbladder from the liver, revealing a tense, stone-filled, and highly inflamed gallbladder. The specimen is prepared for removal.
The surgeon places the gallbladder into a specimen bag for removal, completing the robotic cholecystectomy procedure.
